title: Writing - Part 1: Globalisation and Cultural Identity
description: Your class has attended a seminar on whether globalisation is a threat to local cultures and traditions or a force for positive exchange and connection.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the perspectives in your notes. You should explain which perspective you find most convincing, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Global brands and media are turning every city in the world into a copy of every other city.
Exposure to other cultures makes us more open-minded and empathetic as a society.
Globalisation mainly benefits wealthy nations while exploiting the culture and labour of poorer ones.

structure.introduction: Introduce the debate over globalisation's impact on cultural diversity and local identity.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the argument that globalisation leads to cultural homogenisation.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the counter-argument that global interconnection promotes enriching cultural exchange.
structure.conclusion: Conclude by evaluating which perspective best captures the reality of globalisation's cultural impact.

content.modelAnswer:
Globalisation—the accelerating interconnectedness of economies, societies, and cultures—is one of the defining features of contemporary life. Its impact on cultural diversity has generated intense debate. This essay will examine the argument that globalisation erodes distinct cultural identities and the counter-claim that it instead creates valuable cross-cultural exchange.

The case for cultural homogenisation is difficult to dismiss. In city centres from Seoul to São Paulo, one encounters the same global fast-food chains, streaming platforms, and fashion brands. This commercial uniformity is frequently accompanied by the dominance of English as a global language, which gradually marginalises minority tongues and the unique worldviews they encode. Critics argue that when the outward trappings of a culture—its music, food, and aesthetic—are absorbed and commodified by global markets, the deeper traditions and values that give a community its distinct character become vulnerable to dilution.

Proponents of globalisation, however, argue that cultural exchange is a fundamentally creative and enriching process. History demonstrates that cultures have always evolved through contact with one another, and some of the most vibrant contemporary art forms—from jazz to hip-hop to telenovelas—emerged precisely from the collision of different traditions. Access to global media also allows minority cultures to reach worldwide audiences, giving them a platform they would otherwise lack.

In conclusion, I find the cultural homogenisation argument more persuasive. While exchange can be enriching, it rarely occurs between equal partners. When one culture possesses vastly more economic power than another, what looks like exchange can in practice be absorption, and the loss of genuine diversity is a real and irreversible cost.
